title: Cloud Security Awareness
description: This module introduces the fundamental concepts of cloud security as defined by ISO 27017. Participants will gain an understanding of the shared responsibility model, cloud service delivery models, cloud-specific threats, and the governance considerations essential to secure cloud adoption.

sections.title: The Shared Responsibility Model
sections.content:
The shared responsibility model is a foundational concept in cloud security that delineates the security obligations of the cloud service provider (CSP) and the cloud service customer (CSC). Understanding this division of responsibility is essential for ensuring that no security gaps exist between what the provider manages and what the customer must address.

In general terms, the CSP is responsible for the security of the cloud infrastructure itself, including the physical data centres, network hardware, hypervisors, and the platform services it offers. The CSC retains responsibility for securing its own data, applications, identity and access management configurations, and operating system patches where applicable.

The precise allocation of responsibilities varies depending on the cloud service model in use. In an Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) arrangement, the customer assumes a greater share of security responsibility compared to a Software as a Service (SaaS) model, where the provider manages most of the technology stack.

Organisations must ensure that the shared responsibility model is clearly documented and understood by all relevant stakeholders. Ambiguity in the allocation of responsibilities is a common source of security incidents in cloud environments, and regular reviews of the model should be conducted whenever service configurations or contractual arrangements change.

sections.title: Cloud Service Models
sections.content:
Cloud computing services are typically delivered through three primary models: Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS), and Software as a Service (SaaS). Each model offers a different level of abstraction and correspondingly shifts the balance of security responsibility between the provider and the customer.

IaaS provides virtualised computing resources over the internet, including virtual machines, storage, and networking. The customer retains control over the operating system, middleware, and applications, and must therefore manage patching, configuration hardening, and application-level security controls.

PaaS delivers a managed platform on which customers can develop, run, and manage applications without the complexity of maintaining the underlying infrastructure. Whilst the provider manages the operating system and runtime environment, the customer remains responsible for the security of the application code, data, and access controls.

SaaS provides fully managed applications accessible via the internet, with the provider handling virtually all aspects of the technology stack. The customer's security responsibilities are primarily limited to user access management, data classification, and ensuring that the service configuration aligns with organisational security policies. Regardless of the model, the customer always retains responsibility for the security of its own data.

sections.title: Cloud-Specific Threats
sections.content:
Whilst cloud environments share many of the same threats as traditional IT infrastructure, several threat categories are amplified or unique to cloud deployments. Understanding these threats is critical to implementing effective security controls and maintaining the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of organisational assets.

Data breaches remain the most significant concern in cloud environments. The multi-tenant nature of cloud infrastructure means that a vulnerability in the provider's platform could potentially expose data belonging to multiple customers. Misconfigured storage buckets, inadequate access controls, and insecure application programming interfaces (APIs) are among the most common causes of cloud data breaches.

Account hijacking, whether through credential theft, phishing, or exploitation of authentication weaknesses, can grant an attacker full access to cloud resources. The centralised nature of cloud management consoles means that a single compromised account can have far-reaching consequences across the entire cloud estate.

Insider threats, both from within the CSP and the customer organisation, represent a persistent risk. Additionally, denial of service attacks targeting cloud services can disrupt availability, and insecure interfaces or APIs can provide attack vectors that bypass traditional perimeter defences. Organisations must adopt a defence-in-depth approach that addresses each of these threat categories through layered technical and organisational controls.

sections.title: Data Residency and Sovereignty
sections.content:
Data residency refers to the physical or geographic location where data is stored, whilst data sovereignty concerns the legal jurisdiction that governs that data. In cloud environments, where data may be replicated across multiple regions and data centres, understanding and controlling data residency is a critical governance requirement.

Many jurisdictions impose legal or regulatory requirements that mandate certain categories of data be stored within specific geographic boundaries. For example, organisations subject to UK data protection legislation must ensure that personal data transferred outside the United Kingdom benefits from appropriate safeguards, such as adequacy decisions, standard contractual clauses, or binding corporate rules.

Cloud service providers typically offer region selection capabilities that allow customers to specify the geographic location of their data. However, organisations must verify that metadata, backups, logs, and cached copies are also subject to the same residency controls, as these ancillary data stores are sometimes overlooked.

Organisations should conduct thorough due diligence on their cloud providers' data residency commitments, including reviewing contractual terms, auditing data flow diagrams, and monitoring for any changes to the provider's infrastructure that might affect data location. Failure to maintain appropriate data residency controls can result in regulatory non-compliance and significant reputational harm.

sections.title: Cloud Access Management
sections.content:
Access management in cloud environments requires a robust and disciplined approach to identity governance, authentication, and authorisation. The distributed nature of cloud services, combined with the potential for remote access from any location, makes effective access control a cornerstone of cloud security.

Organisations should implement the principle of least privilege across all cloud resources, ensuring that users and service accounts are granted only the minimum permissions necessary to perform their designated functions. Role-based access control (RBAC) provides a scalable mechanism for managing permissions, and should be complemented by regular access reviews and certification campaigns.

Multi-factor authentication (MFA) should be enforced for all user accounts, with particular emphasis on privileged and administrative accounts. Federated identity management, using protocols such as SAML or OpenID Connect, enables centralised authentication and reduces the proliferation of credentials across multiple cloud services.

Monitoring and auditing of access activities is essential for detecting unauthorised or anomalous behaviour. Cloud providers typically offer native logging and monitoring capabilities that should be integrated with the organisation's security information and event management (SIEM) platform. Automated alerting for suspicious access patterns, such as logins from unusual locations or access outside normal working hours, provides an important layer of detective control.

title: Cloud Security Controls Implementation
description: This module provides detailed guidance on implementing technical security controls in cloud environments in accordance with ISO 27017. Participants will learn how to secure virtual infrastructure, monitor cloud activity, manage cloud incidents, and plan for service transitions.

sections.title: Virtual Machine Security
sections.content:
Virtual machines (VMs) form the backbone of many cloud deployments and must be secured with the same rigour as physical servers. ISO 27017 emphasises the need for cloud customers to apply hardening standards, maintain patching schedules, and implement appropriate monitoring for all virtualised workloads.

VM images should be built from approved, hardened base templates that have been stripped of unnecessary services, default credentials, and unused software packages. These golden images should be version-controlled and regularly updated to incorporate the latest security patches and configuration changes.

Isolation between virtual machines is a critical security requirement in multi-tenant cloud environments. Customers should verify that the cloud provider implements robust hypervisor-level isolation and should deploy additional controls, such as host-based firewalls and intrusion detection systems, to protect individual VMs from lateral movement by attackers.

Lifecycle management of virtual machines must include secure decommissioning procedures. When a VM is no longer required, its associated storage volumes, snapshots, and backups must be securely erased to prevent residual data from being accessible to other tenants or unauthorised parties.

sections.title: Network Segregation in Cloud
sections.content:
Network segregation is a fundamental security control that limits the blast radius of a security incident by restricting communication between different components of the cloud environment. In cloud deployments, this is achieved through virtual networks, subnets, security groups, and network access control lists.

Organisations should design their cloud network architecture with clear boundaries between different tiers of the application stack and between environments of different trust levels. Production, staging, and development environments should be isolated from one another, and sensitive workloads should be placed in dedicated virtual networks with restricted ingress and egress rules.

Micro-segmentation extends the principle of network segregation to the workload level, enabling granular control over east-west traffic within the cloud environment. This approach significantly reduces the ability of an attacker to move laterally between compromised systems and is particularly effective in containerised and microservices architectures.

All network security rules should follow the principle of deny by default, permitting only explicitly authorised traffic flows. Network configurations should be managed as code, subject to version control and peer review, and regularly audited against the organisation's security baseline to identify and remediate configuration drift.

sections.title: Cloud Monitoring and Logging
sections.content:
Comprehensive monitoring and logging are essential for maintaining visibility into the security posture of cloud environments. ISO 27017 requires organisations to implement controls that ensure security-relevant events are captured, retained, and analysed in a timely manner.

Cloud providers offer a range of native logging services that capture API calls, resource changes, authentication events, and network flow data. Organisations should enable these logging capabilities across all cloud accounts and regions, and should configure log retention periods that align with regulatory requirements and the organisation's incident investigation needs.

Log data should be aggregated in a centralised, tamper-resistant repository that is accessible to the security operations team but protected from modification or deletion by cloud administrators. Integration with a SIEM platform enables correlation of events across multiple cloud services and on-premises systems, supporting the detection of sophisticated attack patterns.

Real-time alerting should be configured for high-priority security events, such as changes to identity and access management policies, creation or modification of network security rules, and access to sensitive data stores. Regular review of monitoring dashboards and alert thresholds ensures that the monitoring programme remains effective as the cloud environment evolves.

sections.title: Cloud Incident Management
sections.content:
Incident management in cloud environments presents unique challenges that must be addressed through tailored procedures and close collaboration with cloud service providers. The shared responsibility model means that effective incident response requires clear communication channels and pre-agreed escalation paths between the customer and the provider.

Organisations should develop cloud-specific incident response playbooks that account for the differences between cloud and on-premises environments. These playbooks should cover common cloud incident scenarios, including unauthorised access to cloud management consoles, data exposure through misconfigured storage, and compromise of cloud-hosted applications.

Forensic investigation in cloud environments requires specialised tools and techniques. Traditional disk imaging may not be feasible in virtualised or serverless architectures, and organisations must instead rely on cloud-native forensic capabilities, such as snapshot capture, log analysis, and API-based evidence collection. Incident responders should be trained in these cloud-specific techniques.

Post-incident review processes should evaluate both the technical and governance aspects of the incident. Lessons learned should be incorporated into updated security controls, revised playbooks, and enhanced monitoring rules. Where the incident involved a failure in the shared responsibility model, the review should include a reassessment of the contractual and operational arrangements with the cloud service provider.

sections.title: Exit Strategy and Data Portability
sections.content:
An effective exit strategy ensures that an organisation can transition away from a cloud service provider in an orderly and secure manner, without loss of data, functionality, or continuity of service. ISO 27017 emphasises the importance of planning for service termination as part of the initial cloud adoption process.

Data portability is a key consideration in exit planning. Organisations should ensure that their data is stored in open, standardised formats wherever possible, and should regularly test the ability to export data from the cloud service. Vendor lock-in, whereby proprietary data formats or APIs make migration prohibitively difficult, represents a significant strategic risk that must be managed proactively.

The exit plan should define the steps required to migrate data and workloads to an alternative provider or back to on-premises infrastructure. This includes identifying dependencies on provider-specific services, estimating migration timelines and costs, and establishing criteria for triggering the exit process.

Secure data disposal at the end of the cloud service relationship is equally important. The organisation must obtain assurance from the provider that all data, including backups, snapshots, and cached copies, has been securely erased in accordance with agreed standards. Certificates of destruction should be obtained and retained as part of the organisation's records. Regular testing of the exit plan through tabletop exercises ensures that it remains viable and that all stakeholders understand their roles and responsibilities.
